title: Top 10 Exhaust Fans for Kitchen & Bathroom in India (2026)
description: Top 10 exhaust fans for kitchen and bathroom in India (2026): Atomberg BLDC, Crompton Brizair, plus wall and pedestal picks for tougher ventilation jobs.
author: Elume Editorial
intro: Exhaust fans are the cheapest line on a fan BOM and the first thing tenants complain about when they are under-specified. A bathroom that fogs up, a kitchen that holds cooking smell, a utility room that grows damp patches: all of it traces back to a fan that is too small, too weak or simply missing. In 2026 the category has its own BLDC story too, with Atomberg bringing 11W brushless motors to a segment that ran on 35-60W induction units for decades.

This guide ranks the ventilation options in the Elume catalogue for kitchens, bathrooms and enclosed work areas. The catalogue currently stocks two dedicated exhaust models, the Atomberg Efficio 150mm and the Crompton Brizair 250mm, which take the top two ranks. The remaining eight ranks are filled with ventilation-relevant fans, wall, pedestal, cabin and compact ceiling units, each clearly labelled, because on real sites these are exactly what contractors specify when a wall opening is not available or when a room needs forced air movement rather than extraction.

Judge every pick on three things: the size of the room it must serve, whether you need true extraction through a wall or just strong circulation, and running wattage, since ventilation fans often run for hours at a stretch.

items.name: Efficio Exhaust Fan 150mm (BLDC)
items.body: The clear first choice for bathrooms and small kitchens. Atomberg's BLDC motor draws just 11W at 2000 RPM, roughly a third of a comparable induction exhaust fan, which matters because bathroom fans often run on the light switch for hours daily. The 150mm size suits standard bathroom wall openings, the white finish blends into tiles, and it carries a 1+1 year warranty. At ₹1,448 against a ₹3,350 MRP, it is the modern default exhaust specification for residential projects.
items.bestFor: Bathrooms and small kitchens, lowest running cost

items.name: Brizair 250mm Exhaust Fan
items.body: When the room is a full-size kitchen, a canteen wash area or a larger utility space, step up to the Crompton Brizair 250mm. The bigger sweep moves substantially more air per minute than a 150mm unit, clearing steam, smoke and cooking odour faster, and Crompton's high air delivery design is built exactly for this duty. The white body suits most walls, and at ₹1,230 against a ₹1,850 MRP it is the value pick for kitchens. Pair one Brizair per kitchen with Efficio units in bathrooms for a complete extraction package.

items.name: Turbo Force AC Wall Fan 450mm
items.body: Not an exhaust fan but a wall-mounted circulation fan, ranked here because many kitchens and workshops cannot take a wall opening at all. The Turbo Force mounts high on the wall, throws a strong 450mm stream across the room with three motor speeds, and pushes stale hot air towards an open window or door, which is the practical ventilation route in rented and heritage buildings. A full guard keeps it safe near work areas. At ₹8,378 it is a premium unit, built for commercial kitchens, gyms and shops.
items.bestFor: Rooms where wall openings are impossible

items.name: Aindrila Wall Fan 400mm
items.body: A second wall-fan option, again labelled clearly: this circulates rather than extracts. The Aindrila's 400mm sweep spins at 1350 RPM and delivers 65 m³/min at 55W, enough to keep air moving through a mid-size kitchen, staff room or shop counter. Wall mounting keeps floors clear, which matters in tight commercial spaces, and the three-blade head can be aimed where the heat builds up. At ₹5,340 it undercuts the Turbo Force wall unit while covering most of the same everyday ventilation duty in smaller rooms.
items.bestFor: Compact kitchens and counters needing airflow

items.name: Turbo Force AC Pedestal Fan 450mm
items.body: A pedestal fan in an exhaust guide? Yes, labelled as such, because site ventilation is often temporary. During fit-out, painting and curing, contractors need movable high-volume airflow to dry surfaces and clear fumes, and a 450mm Turbo Force pedestal with adjustable tilt and three speeds does exactly that, then moves to the next floor. The full guard and sturdy base suit rough site conditions. At ₹9,309 it is an equipment purchase rather than a fitting, one or two units serving an entire project through construction.

items.name: Airwynn Cabin Fan
items.body: The Airwynn is a ceiling-mounted cabin fan, a small high-speed unit spinning at 2200 RPM and delivering 60 m³/min, designed for spaces where a full ceiling fan will not fit and a wall opening is unavailable. Think lift lobbies, security cabins, box rooms, shop billing counters and pooja rooms. It keeps air moving vigorously in a tight footprint, which prevents the stuffiness that small enclosed rooms develop. At ₹2,792 it fills a slot in the ventilation BOM that most people forget until handover complaints arrive.
items.bestFor: Cabins, lobbies and tight enclosed rooms

items.name: SS 390 Ceiling Fan 600mm
items.body: Labelled clearly: a compact ceiling fan, not an extractor. The SS 390's 600mm sweep is made for small kitchens, store rooms and passages where a 1200mm fan physically cannot go. It spins at 390 RPM and delivers a strong 230 m³/min at 55W, so it genuinely churns the air in a tight room, pushing heat and moisture towards the exhaust or window instead of letting it sit. At ₹3,037 it pairs naturally with a Brizair or Efficio: the ceiling fan stirs, the exhaust fan expels.
items.bestFor: Small kitchens paired with an extractor

items.name: Efficiencia DX BLDC 900mm
items.body: For mid-size kitchens and utility rooms with a proper ceiling, the 900mm Efficiencia DX brings BLDC economics to the circulation half of the ventilation equation. The brushless motor keeps running cost low even through long cooking hours, it ships with a remote, and the 900mm sweep fits rooms around 8x8ft where 1200mm fans overhang counters. At ₹4,164 against a ₹7,040 MRP, it is the pick when the kitchen fan will run as long as the exhaust fan does and the electricity bill matters.

items.name: Samraat Ceiling Fan 900mm
items.body: The budget version of the same idea: a 900mm compact ceiling fan for kitchens, verandahs and utility areas, labelled as a circulation pick rather than an extractor. The Samraat is a straightforward induction unit in a practical brown finish that hides kitchen grime better than white, and at ₹2,645 it is the cheapest way to get real air movement into a small room. Specify it where budgets are tight, occupancy is light, and the exhaust fan itself is doing the heavy lifting on moisture and smoke.

items.name: Racer 1200mm Fan
items.body: The final slot goes to a high-speed 1200mm ceiling fan, included for large utility spaces, garages and godowns where ventilation means brute air movement across a big floor. The Usha Racer's 400 RPM rating is at the top of the induction class, so it shifts a lot of air fast, helping sheds and store rooms shed heat and damp through open doors. At ₹1,780 it is also the cheapest 1200mm unit here, making it easy to scatter several across a warehouse without denting the budget.

buyingTips: Size extraction to the room: 150mm exhaust fans for bathrooms and small kitchens, 250mm for full-size kitchens and wash areas. An undersized extractor runs constantly and still loses the moisture battle.

Prefer BLDC where the fan runs long hours. The Atomberg Efficio's 11W draw against a typical 35-60W induction exhaust fan adds up over years of daily bathroom use, and the payback is quick at Elume pricing.
Pair extraction with circulation. An exhaust fan clears air at the wall, but a compact ceiling or wall fan pushes room air towards it. Kitchens ventilate noticeably better with both working together.

Check the mounting situation before ordering: wall opening available means a true exhaust fan, no opening means a wall or cabin fan aimed at a window, and temporary site work means a pedestal unit you can move.

Consolidate exhaust, wall and ceiling fans into one bulk order. Elume's wholesale discount applies at 15+ units, and one PO keeps the whole ventilation package on a single delivery and warranty cycle.

faq.q: What size exhaust fan do I need for an Indian bathroom?
faq.a: A 150mm exhaust fan suits standard Indian bathrooms and small kitchens, and matches the wall openings most builders leave. Move to a 250mm unit like the Crompton Brizair for full-size kitchens, larger wash areas or any room where steam and smoke build faster than a small fan can clear.

faq.q: Is a BLDC exhaust fan worth it over a normal one?
faq.a: Usually, yes. The Atomberg Efficio draws 11W where typical induction exhaust fans draw several times that, and bathroom fans often run for hours because they share the light switch. Over a few years of daily use the energy saving covers the price difference, and the quieter motor is a bonus.

faq.q: Can a wall or pedestal fan replace an exhaust fan?
faq.a: Not for true extraction, no. A wall or pedestal fan circulates air inside the room, while an exhaust fan physically expels it. But where no wall opening exists, a wall fan aimed at an open window is the practical fallback, and on construction sites a pedestal fan is often the only workable option.

faq.q: Why do kitchens need both a ceiling fan and an exhaust fan?
faq.a: The exhaust fan removes air at one point on the wall, but heat, steam and smell collect across the whole room. A compact 600mm or 900mm ceiling fan keeps that air moving towards the extractor, so the room clears faster and the exhaust fan does not have to fight still air on its own.

faq.q: Does Elume offer wholesale pricing on exhaust and ventilation fans?
faq.a: Yes. All prices shown are Elume prices below MRP, and orders of 15 or more units attract an additional wholesale discount. Mixing exhaust, wall, cabin and ceiling fans across brands like Atomberg, Crompton, Havells and Usha in one bulk order is the most cost-effective way to buy for a project.
